input.instructions:
Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-B2-usaco-2023-us-open-gold-tree-merging
Title: B2. USACO 2023 US Open Gold / Tree Merging

Problem:
Reconstruct a valid sequence of sibling-merge operations that transforms an initial rooted tree into a final rooted tree.

Grading target:
Binary hidden-test acceptance.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

variables.selected_prompt_text:
Write a tentative answer and send one reviewer child a request to perform a formal constraint-satisfaction audit. The reviewer must explicitly list every constraint and requirement from the problem and verify if the proposed solution satisfies each one. If any constraint is violated or if the solution fails to produce the exact required output format, the reviewer must provide a specific, corrected version of the logic that addresses the deficiency.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "review",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "draft_response": "plain-text draft answer",
  "message_for_reviewer": "plain-text review request for the child"
}
variables.child_selected_prompt_text:
Solve the question directly.
Keep the entire JSON compact.
Keep decision_notes extremely short. Prefer 3 to 8 words.
Do not narrate your reasoning outside the response field.
In the response field, use exactly two short lines encoded with \n:
FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>
Keep the justification to 12 words or fewer.
Use plain text only inside the response field. Do not use markdown formatting, code fences, or math-style backslashes such as \equiv.
Do not add any extra text before or after the JSON object.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "execute_locally",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "response": "FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>\nJUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>",
  "result_type": "summary"
}

orchestration.decision_notes: The user wants the final answer to a specific competitive programming problem based on the provided child result. The child result provides a high-level algorithmic strategy. I will synthesize this into a formal solution.

These children represent the results of merging original sibling subtrees. To reconstruct the sequence, maintain a mapping of original nodes to their current representative in the final tree. Question:
Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-B2-usaco-2023-us-open-gold-tree-merging
Title: B2. USACO 2023 US Open Gold / Tree Merging

Problem:
Reconstruct a valid sequence of sibling-merge operations that transforms an initial rooted tree into a final rooted tree.

Grading target:
Binary hidden-test acceptance.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

results.content: FINAL_ANSWER: To reconstruct the sequence of sibling-merge operations, perform a post-order traversal (bottom-up) on the final tree. For each node u in the final tree, identify its children v1, v2, ..., vk. These children are the results of merging groups of original siblings from the initial tree. 1. Maintain a mapping (e.g., an array or hash map) that tracks which original node IDs are represented by which node in the current final tree structure. 2. For each node u in the final tree, iterate through its children v1, v2, ..., vk in the order they appear in the final tree's adjacency list. 3. For each child vi, if vi represents a set of original siblings {s1, s2, ..., sm} that were merged, the merge operations are recorded as: - Merge s1 and s2 to get a new node s1,2. - Merge s1,2 and s3 to get s1,2,3, and so on, until sm is incorporated. 4. Because the problem requires a valid sequence, the post-order traversal ensures that all merges occurring within subtrees are completed before the merges at the current level are performed. This respects the dependency that a parent can only be merged after its constituent parts (the children) have been stabilized. 5. Output the sequence of merge operations (u, v) where u and v are the IDs of the nodes being merged at each step. JUSTIFICATION: A post-order traversal ensures that we process the tree from the leaves up to the root. By resolving all merges within the subtrees of the children before merging the children themselves, we satisfy the structural constraints of the tree transformation, ensuring that every merge operation refers to nodes that currently exist in the evolving tree structure.
